does: This paper investigates the use of crosscoders—models trained to map between the representations of two different neural networks—as a tool for comparing and understanding differences between models. The authors develop methods to apply crosscoders to the model diffing problem, examining how well these mappings can reveal structural and functional differences between different neural networks. They analyze the effectiveness of crosscoders at identifying where and how models diverge in their learned representations, providing empirical results on the fidelity of cross-model mappings and preliminary findings about what crosscoders reveal about model similarity and differences.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature around mechanistic interpretability and model comparison techniques. It extends prior research on crosscoders themselves, which have been developed to study alignment and correspondence between model representations, and connects to broader work on understanding neural network representations and probing tasks. The paper engages with existing approaches to model diffing and comparison, including feature attribution methods and representation similarity analysis, positioning crosscoders as a complementary approach that leverages learned mappings between model spaces rather than hand-designed similarity metrics.
enables: This preliminary exploration opens avenues for using crosscoders as a systematic tool for model analysis and comparison. Future work could develop more sophisticated crosscoder-based diffing pipelines for identifying behavioral differences, debugging model failures, and understanding how architectural or training choices affect learned representations. The approach could facilitate transfer learning analysis, model merging, and interpretability research by providing fine-grained insight into representational divergence. Results may inform techniques for steering model behavior, detecting unwanted model modifications, and comparing safety properties across different model variants.
